critique.summary: The video titled 'It Took Me 30 Years to Solve This VFX Problem – Green Screen Problem' by Corridor Crew discusses longstanding challenges with green screen technology in visual effects and explores new solutions involving neural networks and procedural generation. The creator reflects on the transparency and chroma key issues that have persisted in VFX and presents initial results of their approach.
mainPoints: Green screens have inherent problems, particularly with transparency and chroma keying.
Previous solutions to green screen issues have been imperfect and incomplete.
The creator has spent 30 years working on solving these VFX problems.
New approaches using neural networks and procedural generation are being explored to improve green screen compositing.
Initial results of these new methods are shared and discussed.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about green screen challenges and the use of neural networks in VFX are consistent with known industry issues and recent technological trends. Corridor Crew is a recognized content creator in the VFX community, and their discussion aligns with common knowledge about chroma key limitations and emerging AI applications. However, the video appears to be a personal project or exploration rather than a peer-reviewed or industry-validated breakthrough. There is no independent verification or multiple sources cited within the video description or transcript to confirm the definitive resolution of these problems.
considerations: The video does not provide independent expert opinions or external validation of the claimed solution.
It lacks detailed technical data or comparative analysis with existing green screen technologies.
The timeline of '30 years' may be a personal reference rather than an industry-wide timeline.
The effectiveness and practical application of the new neural network approach remain to be seen in professional VFX pipelines.
Potential limitations or challenges of the new method are not fully discussed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the challenges and innovations in green screen and VFX keying technology, including the use of synthetic data, hardware requirements, and production practices. Commenters share insights on technical details, historical context, and practical experiences on set lighting and post-production.

comments.author: dylan604
comments.text:
The sad thing about this is the problems encountered during post from the production team saying "fix it post" during the shoot. I've been on set for green screen shoots where the lighting was not done properly. I watched the gaffer walk across the set taking readings from his meter before saying the lighting was good. I flip on the waveform and told him it was not even (which never goes down well when camera dept tells the gaffer it's not right). He put up an argument, went back and took measurements again before repeating it was good. I flipped the screen around and showed him where it was obviously not even. A third set of meter readings and he starts adjust lights. Once the footage was in post, the fx team commented about how easy the keys were because of the even lighting.

The problem is that the vast majority of people on set have no clue what is going on in post. To the point, when the budget is big enough, a post supervisor is present on production days to give input so "fixing it in post" is minimized. When there is no budget, you'll see situations just like in the first 30 seconds of TFA's video. A single lamp lighting the background so you can easily see the light falling off and the shadows from wrinkles where the screen was just pulled out of the bag 10 minutes before shooting. People just don't realize how much light a green screen takes. They also fail to have enough space so they can pull the talent far enough off the wall to avoid the green reflecting back onto the talent's skin.

TL;DR They solved something to make post less expensive because they cut corners during production.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed, experience-based explanation of common production issues affecting green screen quality, highlighting the importance of proper lighting and collaboration between departments.

comments.author: Springtime
comments.text: In an earlier video they made a couple years back about Disney's sodium vapor technique Paul Debevec suggested he was considering creating a dataset using a similar premise: filming enough perfectly masked references to be able to train models to achieve better keying. So it was interesting seeing Corridor tackle this by instead using synthetic data.
comments.analysis: This comment references a credible source and accurately contrasts traditional dataset creation with Corridor's synthetic data approach, adding valuable context to the discussion.

comments.author: diacritical
comments.text:
From ~04:10 till 05:00 they talk about sodium-vapor lights and how Disney has the exclusive rights to use it. From what I read the knowledge on how to make them is a trade secret, so it's not patented. Seems weird that it would be hard to recreate something from the 1950's.

I also wonder how many hours were wasted by people who had to use inferior technology because Disney kept it secret. Cutting out animals and objects from the background 1 frame at a time seems so mindnumbingly boring.
comments.analysis: The comment correctly identifies the nature of Disney's sodium vapor technology as a trade secret and raises a valid point about the impact of secrecy on industry progress.

comments.author: jayd16
comments.text:
As far as alternatives, I wonder if anyone has tried a screen that cycles through colors in a known sequence. Using this modulating-color screen, it might actually be easier to separate the subject because you get around the "green shirt over green screen" problem. You might even be able to use a time sampling to correct the light cast on the subject from the screen as you would have a full spectrum of response.

I could also imagine using polarized light as the backdrop as well.
comments.analysis: This is a creative and technically plausible suggestion that could address common green screen challenges, though it lacks direct evidence or examples.

comments.author: vsviridov
comments.text: The community has managed to drastically lower hardware requirements, but so far I think only Nvidia cards are supported, so as an AMD owner I'm still missing out :(
comments.analysis: This comment accurately reflects current hardware support limitations for many AI-based tools, providing useful information for potential users.

comments.author: superjan
comments.text: Watched this a few days ago. The video is light on technical details, except maybe that they used CGI to generate training data.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a concise critique of the source video, noting the lack of depth in technical explanation while confirming a key point.

comments.author: IshKebab
comments.text:
Pretty impressive results! Seems like someone has even made a GUI for it: https://github.com/edenaion/EZ-CorridorKey

Still Python unfortunately.
comments.analysis: This comment provides practical information about tool accessibility, useful for users interested in trying the software.

comments.author: ralusek
comments.text: I'm a software engineer that, like the vast majority of you, uses AI/agents in my workflow every day. That being said, I have to admit that it feels a little weird to hear someone who does not write code say that they built something, without even mentioning that they had an agent build it (unless I missed that).
comments.analysis: This comment expresses a subjective opinion about transparency in tool development, which is speculative without further evidence.

comments.author: MrVitaliy
comments.text: Anyone tried using lidar and just cut/measure distance to the object?
comments.analysis: This is a relevant technical question that could inspire alternative approaches, though it does not provide evidence or results.

comments.author: amelius
comments.text: There's still a bug: the glass with water does not distort the checker pattern in the background at 24:12.
comments.analysis: This comment identifies a specific visual inconsistency in the video, indicating careful observation.

comments.author: amelius
comments.text: Is it a coincidence that the result is stable between subsequent frames?
comments.analysis: This comment raises a valid technical curiosity about the stability of the keying results, encouraging further investigation.

comments.author: swframe2
comments.text: The model in this repo seems pretty good: https://github.com/xuebinqin/DIS
comments.analysis: Providing a resource link is helpful, though the comment lacks additional context or evaluation.

comments.author: Computer0
comments.text: Looking forward to trying it out, 8gb of vram or unified memory required!
comments.analysis: This comment shares practical hardware requirements, useful for potential users.
